statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, implement the in-order traversal algorithm. The function should traverse the tree in the order of left, root, right and return a list of node values.
statement.inputFormat: The binary tree consists of nodes with values. You can use a binary tree node class or an array to represent the tree structure.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a list of node values in the order of in-order traversal.
statement.notes: You can modify the existing tree or create a copy of the tree. You can also use a recursive approach or an iterative approach.

examples.input: Example input (matching the stdin format that main wrapper expects)

hints.text: Identify the root node and recursively traverse the left subtree.
Modify the recursive traversal to visit the current node after traversing the left subtree.

editorial.title: In-Order Traversal
editorial.intuition: The in-order traversal algorithm can be implemented recursively or iteratively. We can use a stack to keep track of nodes to visit.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves recursively visiting each node and updating the result list.
editorial.betterSolution: The iterative approach uses a stack to keep track of nodes to visit. We can use a while loop to traverse the stack.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a recursive approach and iterating over the result list to remove duplicate values.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be verified by checking the order of node values in the result list.
commonMistakes: In-order traversal is the same as pre-order traversal
Recursive function calls are not properly handled

testCases.input: First line count, second line elements (stdin match)
